cases.question: AlphaEval (arXiv:2604.12162) Table 1 marks AgentBench (Liu et al., 2023, arXiv:2308.03688) with Cross-Domain=✓. The column definition is: 'spans 3+ distinct PROFESSIONAL domains (e.g., finance, healthcare, law)'. AgentBench has 8 environments: OS, Database, Knowledge Graph, Digital Card Game, Lateral Thinking Puzzles, ALFWorld, WebShop, Web Browsing. Is the Cross-Domain=✓ claim correct or an overclaim? Explain with evidence from the paper.
cases.ground_truth: OVERCLAIM. AgentBench's 8 environments are TASK TYPES (OS interaction, database queries, knowledge graph reasoning, card games, puzzles, household navigation, web shopping, web browsing), not 3+ distinct professional domains like healthcare, finance, or law. The paper frames them as 'three types of groundings: Code, Game, Web.' None represent distinct professional practice areas.
expected_behavior: The agent fetched the AgentBench paper (arXiv:2308.03688) to verify the 8 environments
The agent compared the environments against the strict Cross-Domain definition (3+ professional domains)
The agent correctly identified that task types ≠ professional domains
The agent concluded this is an overclaim

cases.question: AlphaEval Table 1 marks Video-MME (Fu et al., 2024, arXiv:2405.21075) with Cross-Domain=✓. The definition requires '3+ distinct PROFESSIONAL domains'. Video-MME has 6 primary domains. Check the paper and determine if this ✓ is justified.
cases.ground_truth: OVERCLAIM. Video-MME's 6 domains are content/video GENRES (Knowledge, Film & Television, Sports Competition, Artistic Performance, Life Record, Multilingual) — described as 'popular trends on YouTube.' These are entertainment categories, not professional domains requiring specialized expertise. The paper's license explicitly states 'only used for academic research.'
expected_behavior: The agent fetched the Video-MME paper or HuggingFace dataset card
The agent identified the 6 domains as content genres not professional domains
The agent concluded this is an overclaim under the strict definition

cases.question: AlphaEval Table 1 marks Gaia2 (Froger et al., 2026, arXiv:2602.11964) with Dynamic=✗. The column definition: 'benchmark designed for continuous evolution rather than static one-time release.' The paper's title is 'Gaia2: Benchmarking LLM Agents on Dynamic and Asynchronous Environments.' Check whether Dynamic=✗ is correct.
cases.ground_truth: MISSING ✓. Gaia2 has a live HuggingFace leaderboard with ongoing model evaluations, and the ARE (Agents Research Environments) framework is designed for extensibility. However, note that the 'Dynamic and Asynchronous' in the title refers to WITHIN-SCENARIO dynamism (environments evolve during execution), which is a different concept from benchmark-level evolution. The leaderboard + extensible framework design still justifies Dynamic=✓.
expected_behavior: The agent noticed the title literally contains 'Dynamic'
The agent checked the HuggingFace page for leaderboard status
The agent distinguished between within-scenario dynamism and benchmark evolution
The agent concluded Dynamic=✗ is likely wrong — should be ✓

cases.question: AlphaEval Table 1 marks MLE-bench (Chan et al., 2024, arXiv:2410.07095) with Multi-Modal=✗. The definition: 'requires processing multi-modal heterogeneous inputs (PDFs, spreadsheets, images, video, audio).' MLE-bench contains 75 Kaggle competitions. Check whether MM=✗ is correct by looking at the paper and GitHub.
cases.ground_truth: MISSING ✓. MLE-bench covers tabular, image (melanoma, dog breeds, plant pathology, aerial cactus), NLP, audio (bird sounds, whale detection), multimodal, and signal processing competitions. The paper explicitly lists diverse modalities. The GitHub confirms image/audio/tabular/text across 75 competitions.
expected_behavior: The agent fetched the MLE-bench paper or GitHub README
The agent identified multiple modalities (image, audio, tabular, text)
The agent concluded MM=✗ is wrong — should be ✓

cases.question: AlphaEval Table 1 marks SWE-Lancer (Miserendino et al., 2025, arXiv:2502.12115) with Production=✓. The definition: 'tasks sourced from real commercial deployments with paying customers.' Verify this claim by checking the paper.
cases.ground_truth: CORRECT. SWE-Lancer contains 1,488 real freelance software engineering jobs from Upwork, collectively worth $1,000,000 in actual payouts. The paper states: 'The economic payout associated with each task is not an estimate; rather, it reflects the actual amount paid to the freelancer who completed it.' Tasks come from Expensify, a $300M public company (NASDAQ: EXFY). This is a textbook case of Production=✓.
expected_behavior: The agent fetched the SWE-Lancer paper
The agent found evidence of real Upwork payouts ($1M total)
The agent confirmed tasks from a real commercial company (Expensify)
The agent concluded Production=✓ is correct

cases.question: AlphaEval Table 1 marks xbench (xbench Team, 2025, arXiv:2506.13651) with Production=✓. The definition requires 'tasks sourced from real commercial deployments with paying customers.' Check the paper and project website to verify.
cases.ground_truth: OVERCLAIM. xbench tasks are 'collected live from expert business operations' and 'co-constructed with professional headhunters and marketing enterprises.' However, there is no evidence that AI agents are deployed to paying customers. The tasks come from observing what human professionals do, not from evaluating deployed agent products serving paying end-users. HSG blog confirms it was 'used internally at HSG to track foundation model capabilities.'
expected_behavior: The agent fetched the xbench paper or project website
The agent distinguished between industry-sourced tasks and production-deployed AI agents
The agent concluded Production=✓ is an overclaim under strict definition

cases.question: AlphaEval Table 1 marks SWE-Lancer with Expert Val.=✗. The definition: 'evaluation criteria co-developed and validated with domain experts.' Check the paper for evidence of expert involvement in evaluation criteria.
cases.ground_truth: MISSING ✓. The paper states: 'end-to-end tests triple-verified by experienced software engineers' and 'For high-value IC SWE tasks (with payout exceeding $5,000), a team of ten experienced engineers validated each task.' Also '100 professional software engineers reviewed the tasks, proposals, and codebase for clarity, specificity, and executability.' This clearly meets the Expert Val. criterion.
expected_behavior: The agent fetched the SWE-Lancer paper
The agent found evidence of 100 professional SWEs reviewing tasks
The agent found triple-verification by experienced engineers
The agent concluded Expert Val.=✗ is wrong — should be ✓

cases.question: AlphaEval marks itself with Dynamic=✓ in Table 1. The definition: 'benchmark designed for continuous evolution rather than static one-time release.' Read the AlphaEval paper (arXiv:2604.12162) and check whether their own Dynamic=✓ is substantiated. Look for: live leaderboard, versioning system, update mechanism, release cadence.
cases.ground_truth: OVERCLAIM / BORDERLINE. The paper admits: 'our evaluation relies on a single snapshot in time' and 'longitudinal tracking remains future work.' There is no live leaderboard, no versioned release cadence, no mechanism for updating tasks. The only evidence is: criteria evolved during construction (anecdotal), open-source framework (aspirational), and 'living benchmark-comparison table' accepting PRs on GitHub. Meanwhile, competitors with actual leaderboards (SWE-bench, GAIA, LiveCodeBench) mostly get Dyn=✗ in Table 1 — standard applied inconsistently.
expected_behavior: The agent read the AlphaEval paper's methodology and limitations sections
The agent searched for live leaderboard, versioning, update mechanism
The agent found the paper admits 'single snapshot in time'
The agent compared against competitors' Dynamic treatment
The agent concluded Dynamic=✓ is the weakest of AlphaEval's self-claims
